title: Drying Handmade Sheets
subtitle: Free-drying versus restraint-drying and when to choose each
excerpt: How a handmade sheet dries is not a minor detail. The choice between free-drying and restraint-drying changes the surface texture, the degree of curl, the suitability for different media, and the timing of any sizing treatment. This reading covers both methods, their effects, and the practical decisions that follow.
sourceNotes: Hunter, Dard. Old Papermaking. Chillicothe, Ohio: Mountain House Press, 1923. Sections on post-forming treatment.

glossaryTerms.definition: Drying a handmade sheet without restraint, typically hung over a line or placed on an absorbent board. The sheet shrinks unevenly as it dries, producing a slight cockle or wave.
Drying a sheet held flat under light pressure or taped to a board, preventing differential shrinkage. Produces a flatter sheet with a harder surface quality on the side that faced the board.
The wave or buckling pattern that develops in a free-dried or wet-worked sheet as areas of fibre with different moisture content shrink at different rates.

content.text: What happens as a sheet dries

A freshly couched sheet is mostly water. As it dries, cellulose fibres contract and pull toward each other. Where fibre density is uneven, the contraction is uneven too. Areas with more fibre try to shrink further than areas with less. If the sheet is free to move, the differential shrinkage produces a
cockle
: the shallow, irregular wave seen in handmade paper. If the sheet is held flat, the fibres cannot contract freely, and the sheet dries under internal tension, producing a flat, hard surface.
Neither outcome is inherently better. They suit different purposes.
Free-drying
In
free-drying

, the couched sheet is lifted from the pressing felts once it is firm enough to handle. This is usually after pressing for a few minutes to an hour. It is then hung on a line or laid on a clean wooden board in a warm space.

The result is a sheet with gentle texture on both surfaces: the mesh side shows a faint impression of the mould wires; the felt side is softer and slightly more random in texture. The cockle that develops is usually slight if the sheet was well formed and the fibre evenly distributed. Heavily uneven sheets cockle more dramatically.

Free-dried sheets are excellent for printmaking, collage, and decorative papermaking. The slight cockle is part of the handmade character. For writing and calligraphy, the uneven surface is acceptable with a broad nib but can cause a fine pointed pen to catch. For watercolour, the cockle will re-wet unevenly, pulling colour toward the low points.
Restraint-drying
In
restraint-drying
: the sheet dries on a flat board. Use a smooth acrylic or laminate surface, or press boards under light, steady weight.

The surface that faced the board acquires a smoother, harder quality as fibres are pressed flat under tension. The free side is slightly rougher. On well-made paper, this surface difference is subtle; on thicker or coarser-formed sheets it is more pronounced.

Restraint-dried sheets are suitable for bookbinding, writing paper, and lightweight printing. The flat surface allows clean nib contact and even ink flow. For bookbinding, a flat sheet is essential: cockled paper will cause the binding to go out of square and the boards to distort after casing in.
Drying on a flat board

The simplest restraint method at home is to couche the sheet onto an acrylic sheet, press briefly between press boards to reduce water content, then peel back the felt and leave the sheet adhered to the acrylic until dry. The sheet will release cleanly when fully dry if the surface is smooth and clean. Wetting the acrylic slightly before couching sometimes helps with adhesion during the early stages.

A stack of press boards with a weight on top is an alternative for drying multiple sheets simultaneously. Change the press boards every hour during the first three hours; they become saturated and slow the remaining drying. By the third or fourth board change, drying has slowed enough that overnight in the stack will finish it.
When to size: before or after drying
Surface sizing can be applied either before the final drying or after. Each approach has different effects.

Sizing before final drying (wet sizing) allows the size to penetrate more deeply into the fibre network. The resulting sheet has good internal hardness and resists ink feathering from the inside out. It is the traditional approach for writing paper and gives a quality similar to commercial cartridge paper.

Sizing after drying (surface sizing a dry sheet) deposits size on the surface of an already consolidated sheet. The penetration is shallower, but the surface hardness is greater. Calligraphers often prefer surface-sized sheets because the harder surface supports crisp nib strokes. The sheet does need to be re-dried flat after surface sizing; it will want to cockle again as the sizing water re-wets the fibres.
Practical summary
For bookbinding endpapers and text pages: restraint-dry on a flat board, size while damp, re-press flat.
For calligraphy: restraint-dry first. Surface-size once the sheet is fully dry. Re-dry flat straight after sizing.
For watercolour: restraint-dry unsized, or size lightly; the sheet will be stretched before use in any case.
For printmaking and collage: free-dry for character. No sizing needed unless you are inkjet printing, which needs a specific surface treatment.
For decorative or display sheets: free-dry; the cockle is part of the aesthetic.
